content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIRPOLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
April 1,
1996
The Honorable Grace E. .Napolitano
Assemblymember, Fifty-Eighth District
State Capitol
Post Office Box 942849
Sacramento,
California
94249—0001
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-112
Dear Assemblymember Napolitano:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
“gift” and “contribution” provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
You are cosponsoring a conference about international trade
issues with various public and private entities.
Will any of
these entities have made a gift or contribution to you?
CONCLUSION
The payments will not be considered gifts or contributions,
except payments for food provided free of charge to conferees
that is behested by you or your staff.
FACTS
A large number of your constituents and a host of private
sector organizations, trade officials from other nations in the
Americas,
U.S.
and state trade officials,
and business
representatives have requested that you cosponsor an international
trade conference this summer to accomplish the following purposes:
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SLITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—1l2
Page
2
1.
Match potential exporters and importers from the Greater
Los Angeles area who lack the knowledge and required expertise to
establish and expand a trading relationship with foreign buyers
and sellers, with the proprietors of businesses and trade
officials from nations in the Pacific Rim, North, Central and
South America who are interested in purchasing and selling goods
and services from businesses in this area.
2.
Establish trade alliances through the adoption of “power
in regional partnership agreements,” and convey information on
rules governing trade, export financing, cultural sensitivity,
export pricing,
and agricultural/food export to participants in
the conference.
3.
Establish a foundation for new trade relationships which
will stimulate the economy of the Greater Los Angeles area by
opening new international markets for businesses which previously
have not participated in trade in the Americas and Pacific Rim.
A steering committee of these constituencies has been meeting
on a regular basis and has made numerous suggestions about how the
event should be conducted.
The steering committee has suggested
that you conduct a two—day trade conference held at a local
community college (such as Cerritos College in Norwalk)
in their
student center,
gymnasium,
student classrooms,
and outdoor quad
area.
Parking would be provided on campus or off.
You would like to offer the event free of charge to anyone
from the public who would like to attend,
including
representatives from small and medium—sized companies, students,
importers, exporters, shippers, etc.
You would like to invite state, federal,
local agencies,
and
you would like to advertise it as being cosponsored by Cerritos
College,
a federal agency
(such as the U.S.
Agency for
International Development), private sector corporations, local
chambers of commerce, utility companies,
and others.
These
organizations would have varying degrees of support for the event.
Some sponsors would merely assist in outreach so that you would
have a large number of participants attend the event.
Other
sponsors would provide staff time to attend planning meetings
(steering committee meetings), assist with setup,
make phone
calls,
locate companies outside the U.S.
who are interested in
importing and/or exporting goods and services, organize reduced
hotel rates,
coordinate food, etc.
The event would allow small and medium—sized businesses to
have the opportunity to participate in educational break-out
sessions during which state, federal,
local agencies,
and private
sector companies can provide information about aspects of
international trade (such as the ABC’s of exporting, freight
forwarding, etc.).
You would have one or more keynote speakers
(from the public or private sectors) to provide information to the


File No.
A—96—112
Page
3
group as a whole about international trade
(such as Big Emerging
Markets, California’s Role as an Export Intensive State, etc.,).
You would like to offer every attendee free handouts and
information about international trade such as books or other
written materials generated by state, federal,
local agencies, or
private sector companies related to international trade.
One
example is the International Resources Guide published by the
State Trade and Commerce Agency and the California Chamber of
Commerce.
Two private sector entities have offered to coordinate food
for the event.
They would organize local restaurants and others
to provide free coffee, tea, water,
soda,
and other drinks.
They
have also offered to coordinate a continental breakfast and lunch
to be offered free to all attendees.
Two private sector firms have offered to pay for the printing
of the fliers, advertising the event and the cost of postage to
mail them.
As a service, to your small and medium—sized companies,
you would like to-assemble a book of company profiles to be sent
to all of the countries of the Americas and Pacific Rim.
These
profiles would be gathered from the local businesses, typeset,
complied into a book, printed,
and distributed to attendees and
other countries.
The steering committee has suggested that you
negotiate a reduced rate at -a local hotel for attendees.
Two
steering committee members have offered to provide computerized
pre— and on—site registration.
The steering committee has suggested that you hold three
teleconferences prior to the trade conference in order to provide
essential information for successful trading between the U.S.
and
specific foreign countries.
These teleconferences are also
designed to stimulate interest in international trade in general
and the conference in particular.
The first of these
teleconferences would be conducted at a local university and
broadcast to Mexico.
Participants would include small and medium—
sized companies from the two countries who would discuss importing
and exporting trade areas such as medical,
biotechnology,
food and
agriculture, etc.
These teleconferences would be taped and
rebroadcast on cable public access channels,
down—linked to
community colleges and other universities,
and made available at
public libraries.
The steering committee would like to hold a reception
immediately following the first day of the trade conference.
The
purpose would be to allow small and medium—sized businesses to
network with each other in a one—on—one fashion.
Your office
would not be hosting this event, but you would be invited to
attend and your staff may be asked to facilitate in the event the
steering committee needed help in the process of coordinating the
event.


File No.
A—96—112
Page
5
Regulation 18215(b) (3) provides that a contribution includes
“[a]ny goods or services received by or behested by a candidate or
committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market
value, unless the discount is given in the regular course of
business to members of the public.”
Regulation 18215(c)
further provides,
in pertinent part, that
the term contribution does not include:
***
(2)
Volunteer personal services or payments
made by a person for his own travel expenses, if
such payments are made voluntarily without any
understanding or agreement that he or she will be
repaid.
(3)
A payment made by an occupant of a home
or office for costs related to any meeting or
fundraising event held in the occupant’s home or
office, if the total cost of the meeting or
fundraising event is $500 or less, exclusive of the
fair rental value of the premises.
(4)
A payment made at the behest of a
candidate,
which is for a communication by the
candidate or any other person, that meets all of
the following:
(i)
Does not contain express advocacy;
(ii)
Does not make reference to the
candidate’s candidacy for elective office, the
candidate’s election campaign, or the candidate’s
or his or her opponent’s qualifications for office;
and
(iii)
Does not solicit contributions to the
candidate or to third persons for use in support of
the candidate or in opposition to the candidate’s
opponent.
Where a payment is not a contribution, it may in some
circumstances be considered a gift.
“Gift” is defined in Section
82028 as any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or
greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in
the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status.
The term “gift” does not include material which serves
primarily to convey information and which is provided for the
purpose of assisting the recipient in the performance of his or
her official duties.
(Section 82028; Regulation 18942.1.)
Of


File No.
A—96—112
Page
4
The steering committee would like to allow local businesses
to have tabletop displays so that they may showcase their products
or services to the foreign buyers.
The steering committee would
like to have signs posted directing the public to the various
functions within the event,
including parking and break-out
sessions.
ANALYSIS
Section 82015 defines “contribution” as any payment,
forgiveness of a loan,
a payment of a loan by a third party,
or an
enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that
full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes.
A payment is made for political purposes if
it is:
(1)
for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates,
or the qualification or
passage of any measure; or
(2)
received by or made at the behest
of a candidate.
2
(Regulation 18215(a).)
Section 82007 defines “candidate,” in pertinent part,
as any
individual who is listed on the ballot for elective office.
An
individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her status
as a candidate until such time as the candidate or his committee
has filed a statement of termination pursuant to Section 84214 and
Regulation 18404.
Thus,
as an elected official,
you are
considered a candidate under Section 82007.
Consequently,
any
payments you behest are presumed to be for political purposes and
will be considered contributions absent an exception.
(Danner
Advice Letter, A—96-039.)
3
2
Regulation 18215 provides:
“A payment is made for political
purposes if it is:
(1)
for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passageof any measure; or
(2)
received by or
made at the behest of the following or any agent thereof:
(A)
a
candidate;
(B)
a controlled committee;
(C)
an official committee
of a political party,
including a state central committee,
county
central committee,
assembly district committee or any subcommittee
of such committee; or
(D)
an organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes,
including,
but not limited to,
a
political action committee established by any membership
organization,
labor union or corporation.
A candidate “behests” a contribution when the contribution is
made “under the control or at the direction of,.
in cooperation,
consultation, coordination,
or concert with, at the request or
suggestion of,
or with the express, prior consent of.”
(Regulation 18225.7(a).)


File No.
A—96—112
Page6
course, whether a payment is a gift or contribution is a factual
question which must be determined on a case—by—case basis.
1.
Private Employers.
Several private sector entities
interested in trade issues will be cosponsoring the event.
The
organizations would provide staff time to set up the event,
participate in break—out sessions, have keynote speakers, provide
computerized registration and organize displays.
If the
individuals are volunteering their time,
we would not consider
such volunteer personal services contributions.
(Regulation
18215(c)(2).)
If they are acting in their official capacities and
are paid by their private employers, they would not be making
contributions so long as their involvement was not “campaign”
related.
(Schmidt Advice Letter,
No.
A—96-106..)
Regulation 18423
describes when payments for the personal services of private
employees may be considered a campaign contribution or
expenditure.
For purposes of Regulation 18423, personal services
are rendered for “political purposes” if they are carried on for
the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action
of the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or
more candidates,
or the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18423(b).)
Based on your facts, the personal services
provided by employees of the businesses attending the trade
conference would not be rendered for political purposes,
as
defined in Regulation 18423(b).
Therefore,
none of these services
will be contributions to you.
2.
Public Employers.
Several public entities will also be
participating in the event and performing the services described
above.
These individuals are presumably acting in their official
capacities and will be paid by their employers.
Their personal
services would not be considered contributions if they meet the
requirements of Regulation 18420, which provides that a government
agency’s services behested by a candidate and provided in
connection with an event that is not a “campaign” activity are not
considered contributions.
Regulation 18420 states that campaign
activities include,
among others, arranging a campaign event,
acting in the capacity of the campaign manager, soliciting,
receiving or acknowledging contributions, or developing, writing,
or distributing campaign literature.
No contribution to you will
result from the personal services performed at the trade
conference.
3.
Location,
Lodging and Admission.
The trade conference
will be held at a local community college.
The college will be
making its premises available for the event free of charge.
You
have not provided any facts indicating whether the school has a
policy of making its facilities available to community groups or
government entities without charge.
If the community college
would normally make its facilities available to the general public
or government officials, the exception for discounts made
available to the general public will apply.
(Regulation
18215(b) (3);
Schmidt Advice Letter,
No. A-96-072.)
No
contribution will result.
You mentioned that you plan to


File No.
A—96—112
Page 7
negotiate a discounted rate at a local hotel for the conferees.
The exception in Regulation 18215(b) (3) will apply so long as you
obtain a discounted rate that the hotel would ordinarily provide
to a group function.
The exception would not apply,
however, if
you obtain a lower rate by virtue of your official status.
Finally, free admission to the event provided to others will not
be a contribution to you.
4.
Publications.
You anticipate that various publications
will be created in conjunction with the event.
You anticipate
free handouts,
information about international trade, fliers
advertising the event,
a book of company profiles and signs
directing the public to various functions and parking at the
event.
In addition,
a teleconference will be videotaped and
rebroadcast.
Such communications would not be considered campaign
contributions if they meet the requirements of Regulation
18215(c) (4).
Regulation 18215(c) (4) provides that communications
are not contributions if they do not contain express advocacy,
do
not make reference to the candidate’s candidacy for elective
office or the candidate’s opponent for elective office and do not
solicit contributions.
5.
Food.
You anticipate donations of food as well.
Two
private sector entities have offered to coordinate food and will
organize local restaurants and others to provide free beverages,
continental breakfast and lunches to all conferees.
In addition,
the steering committee,
in coordination with your staff, will host
a reception immediately following the first day of the conference.
Under your facts, these payments would be considered contributions
to the elected officials who solicited or coordinated the payment
for refreshments absent some exception.
The only possible
exception is in Regulation 18215(c) (3).
However, this exception
will not apply in this case since the event will not be taking
place in the donor’s home or office.
(Schmidt Advice Letter,
No.
A—96—072.)
Since you are cosponsoring the event and your staff is
involved in coordinating the payments, the payments for food will
be contributions to you and must be disclosed as such.
If the
conferees pay for their food,
however,
no contribution will
result.
6.
Gifts.
Because the donated personal services discussed
in paragraphs
1 and 2 above are not provided for political
purposes as defined in Regulations 18420 and 18423, they might
constitute gifts to you and other elected officials instead.
(Section 82028.)
However, the trade-related workshops and
information would be provided primarily to convey information to
the conference attendees and to assist the elected officials in
the performance of their governmental duties.
Thereföre,
such
workshops and information would be considered “informational
material” and not gifts to you or other elected officials.
(Regulation 18942.1.)


File No.
A—96—l12
Page
8
If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Cou
el
By:
Liane
andolph
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak

that is behested by you or your staff.

contributions if they meet the requirements of Regulation
18215(c) (4).
Regulation 18215(c) (4) provides that communications
are not contributions if they do not contain express advocacy,
do
not make reference to the candidate’s candidacy for elective
office or the candidate’s opponent for elective office and do not
solicit contributions.
5.
Food.
You anticipate donations of food as well.
Two
private sector entities have offered to coordinate food and will
organize local restaurants and others to provide free beverages,
continental breakfast and lunches to all conferees.
In addition,
the steering committee,
in coordination with your staff, will host
a reception immediately following the first day of the conference.
Under your facts, these payments would be considered contributions
to the elected officials who solicited or coordinated the payment
for refreshments absent some exception.
The only possible
exception is in Regulation 18215(c) (3).
However, this exception
will not apply in this case since the event will not be taking
place in the donor’s home or office.
(Schmidt Advice Letter,
No.
A—96—072.)
Since you are cosponsoring the event and your staff is
involved in coordinating the payments, the payments for food will
be contributions to you and must be disclosed as such.
If the
conferees pay for their food,
however,
no contribution will
result.
6.
Gifts.
Because the donated personal services discussed
in paragraphs
1 and 2 above are not provided for political
purposes as defined in Regulations 18420 and 18423, they might
constitute gifts to you and other elected officials instead.
(Section 82028.)
However, the trade-related workshops and
information would be provided primarily to convey information to
the conference attendees and to assist the elected officials in
the performance of their governmental duties.
Thereföre,
such
workshops and information would be considered “informational
material” and not gifts to you or other elected officials.
(Regulation 18942.1.)


File No.
A—96—l12

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You are cosponsoring a conference about international trade
issues with various public and private entities.
Will any of
these entities have made a gift or contribution to you?

CONCLUSION: The payments will not be considered gifts or contributions,
except payments for food provided free of charge to conferees
that is behested by you or your staff.
embedding.first_500_words: RAVI MEHTA CHAIRMAN FAIRPOLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION April 1, 1996 The Honorable Grace E. .Napolitano Assemblymember, Fifty-Eighth District State Capitol Post Office Box 942849 Sacramento, California 94249—0001 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-96-112 Dear Assemblymember Napolitano: This is in response to your request for advice regarding the “gift” and “contribution” provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 QUESTIONS You are cosponsoring a conference about international trade issues with various public and private entities. Will any of these entities have made a gift or contribution to you? CONCLUSION The payments will not be considered gifts or contributions, except payments for food provided free of charge to conferees that is behested by you or your staff. FACTS A large number of your constituents and a host of private sector organizations, trade officials from other nations in the Americas, U.S. and state trade officials, and business representatives have requested that you cosponsor an international trade conference this summer to accomplish the following purposes: 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18995. 428 J STREET, SLITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 File No. A—96—1l2 Page 2 1. Match potential exporters and importers from the Greater Los Angeles area who lack the knowledge and required expertise to establish and expand a trading relationship with foreign buyers and sellers, with the proprietors of businesses and trade officials from nations in the Pacific Rim, North, Central and South America who are interested in purchasing and selling goods and services from businesses in this area. 2. Establish trade alliances through the adoption of “power in regional partnership agreements,” and convey information on rules governing trade, export financing, cultural sensitivity, export pricing, and agricultural/food export to participants in the conference. 3. Establish a foundation for new trade relationships which will stimulate the economy of the Greater Los Angeles area by opening new international markets for businesses which previously have not participated in trade in the Americas and Pacific Rim. A steering committee of these constituencies has been meeting on a regular basis and has made numerous suggestions about how the event should be conducted. The steering committee has suggested that you conduct a two—day trade conference held at a local community college (such as Cerritos College in Norwalk) in their student center, gymnasium, student classrooms, and outdoor quad area. Parking would be provided on campus or off. You would like to offer the event free of charge to anyone from the public who would like to attend, including representatives from small and medium—sized companies, students, importers, exporters, shippers, etc. You would like to invite state, federal, local agencies, and you would like to advertise it as being cosponsored by Cerritos College, a federal agency (such as the U.S. Agency for International Development), private sector corporations, local chambers of commerce, utility companies, and others. These organizations would have varying degrees of support for the event. Some
